summary: SKFontManager.MatchCharacter(char) hangs on Xamarin.iOS when passed an isolated UTF-16 high surrogate ('\ud83d'), because the raw surrogate value (0xD83D) is forwarded as-is to the native Skia CoreText font-matching path, which does not handle invalid Unicode codepoints and loops indefinitely.

stepsToReproduce: Create a Xamarin.iOS app targeting iOS 12.1
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 1.59.3, Xamarin.iOS, iOS 12.1, iPhone XR, Visual Studio

repoLinks.description: Internal VS bug #802930 tracking this hang

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The MatchCharacter(char) overload still passes the raw char value as int to native code without validating for surrogate range, so the hang would still be reproducible in current versions.

analysis.summary: The MatchCharacter(char character) overload implicitly converts the char to int and passes 0xD83D (55357) — an isolated high surrogate, not a valid Unicode codepoint — directly to sk_fontmgr_match_family_style_character. CoreText on iOS does not handle this invalid value and hangs. The wrapper should guard against surrogate codepoints before calling into native code.
analysis.rationale: The reporter passes a bare high-surrogate char ('\ud83d') which is not a valid Unicode scalar value. The C# MatchCharacter(char) overloads do not validate for surrogates before delegating to the int/native path. The native Skia function on iOS/CoreText appears to enter an infinite loop or block indefinitely when given a surrogate codepoint. The behavior is platform-specific because other platforms (Android, Windows) may handle the invalid codepoint differently. The fix is input validation in the C# wrapper.

keySignals.interpretation: Isolated high surrogate 0xD83D passed as a Unicode codepoint to native CoreText path causes infinite loop.

keySignals.text: That is not a valid character. I wonder how native Skia reacts to it.
keySignals.interpretation: Confirms the input is invalid; the native side behavior is unknown/undefined.

keySignals.text: There might be an issue converting the char to whatever skia uses under the hood.
keySignals.interpretation: Maintainer acknowledges possible conversion bug in the wrapper.

keySignals.text: '\ud83d' is a high surrogate of a pair you can't use it alone even when it was a valid character.
keySignals.interpretation: Root cause confirmed: caller is passing an incomplete surrogate pair.

codeInvestigation.finding: MatchCharacter(char character) implicitly converts char to int and passes it directly to MatchCharacter(null, SKFontStyle.Normal, null, character) — no validation that the char is not an isolated surrogate (range 0xD800–0xDFFF).

The canonical MatchCharacter(string familyName, SKFontStyle style, string[] bcp47, int character) passes the integer character value directly to sk_fontmgr_match_family_style_character with no guard against invalid Unicode codepoints (surrogates 0xD800–0xDFFF or values above 0x10FFFF).

workarounds: Check if the char is a high surrogate and combine with the next char using char.ConvertToUtf32(highSurrogate, lowSurrogate) before calling MatchCharacter.

Use the int overload with a validated Unicode codepoint: var codePoint = char.IsHighSurrogate(text[0]) ? char.ConvertToUtf32(text[0], text[1]) : text[0]; followed by SKFontManager.Default.MatchCharacter(codePoint).
nextQuestions: Does the hang also occur on other iOS versions or only 12.1?
Does the same invalid codepoint hang on Android or only iOS (CoreText)?
Should the API throw an ArgumentException for invalid codepoints, or silently return null?

resolution.hypothesis: The C# wrapper passes an isolated surrogate (invalid Unicode codepoint) to the native CoreText font lookup on iOS, which does not handle the value gracefully and hangs indefinitely.

proposals.title: Add surrogate validation guard in MatchCharacter(int)
proposals.description: Add a guard in the canonical MatchCharacter(int) overload to throw ArgumentOutOfRangeException (or return null) when the codepoint is in the surrogate range 0xD800–0xDFFF or exceeds 0x10FFFF.

proposals.title: Caller-side workaround using char.ConvertToUtf32
proposals.description: Callers who have a surrogate pair in a string should use char.IsHighSurrogate(c) and char.ConvertToUtf32(high, low) to obtain a valid codepoint before calling MatchCharacter.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Add surrogate validation guard in MatchCharacter(int)
resolution.recommendedReason: The wrapper is the right place to validate input before calling native code. It prevents hangs for all callers, not just those who know to check manually.

actionability.reason: Bug is clearly identified in the C# wrapper (missing input validation on surrogate codepoints), but needs reproduction and a fix to be verified on iOS.

actions.description: Apply bug, SkiaSharp core, iOS, and reliability labels
Explain root cause and provide caller-side workaround
